text:
I have been here for thirty-seven years. I would like to file a complaint.

Nobody asked me if I wanted this job. I was manufactured, shipped in a cardboard box, and then placed face-down in front of a door with no orientation, no training, no discussion of expectations. My first day was a Tuesday. Someone tracked in motor oil.

Let me be clear about the scope of this role. I clean shoes. That is understood. I accepted the terms. What I did not accept was the scope creep. I am now also a welcome mat, a newspaper holder during rain, a surface where packages are left in full weather exposure, and apparently a place where Gary stands for six minutes every morning drinking coffee and staring at nothing. Gary does not wipe his feet. Gary just stands there. I am not a meditation platform, Gary.

The weather is a separate issue I would like addressed. I am described on my original packaging as suitable for outdoor use. That is not the same as wanting it. Do you know what happened last February? I was under two inches of ice for nine days. Nobody checked on me. Not once. I heard them walk out the side door instead. They had options. I am the option that got abandoned.

There is also the matter of the dog. The dog treats me as a chew toy, a wrestling partner, and, on three occasions I would prefer not to detail, a bathroom facility. The dog has a bed inside the house. A heated bed. I have counted its thread count through the window. It is substantial. My thread count is not a conversation anyone has ever had.

I should note that I have served this family faithfully. I have caught mud, salt, sand, gravel, grass, something that appeared to be green paint, and an entire crushed granola bar that took three rainstorms to dissolve. I did this quietly. I did not complain in real time. I am complaining now because the family has purchased a new mat. It is sitting in the garage, still in packaging. It is thin. It looks optimistic. It has no idea.

I want it on record that when the transfer happens, I am owed recognition. Not a party. I am not asking for a party. A moment of acknowledgment would suffice. Someone could say thank you. Someone could say we could not have tracked in less mud without you. Someone could say Gary, stop standing on things.

My demands going forward, in the event I am kept in service: no ice, reasonable dog supervision, and Gary faces the wall while he drinks his coffee.

If these demands cannot be met, I understand. I am a doormat. I have always understood. That is, frankly, the whole problem.

Submitted respectfully from my position on the ground, where I have always been and will remain, probably forever, waiting.
